title: Allan govt unveils $14m rescue package as it braces for AI-driven job losses
summary: The Allan government has announced a $14 million package to help Victorian workers facing job losses due to AI. The plan includes an $8.2 million career rescue scheme and a new TAFE centre to support those in at-risk industries.
ticker_line: Victoria launches $14m AI job rescue package
author: Alex White
heading: AI

title: NAB fixed interest rate hike signals more pain to come for borrowers
summary: National Australia Bank (NAB) has raised its fixed mortgage rates by 15 basis points, indicating potential further interest rate hikes ahead. The lowest fixed rate is now 6.49%, and forecasts suggest rates could rise again by August as the Reserve Bank of Australia continues to combat inflation.
ticker_line: NAB raises fixed mortgage rates amid inflation fight
author: Cameron Micallef
heading: Banking

title: Super not an answer to all your tax issues
summary: Recent budget changes have made superannuation a more tax-friendly investment, but it may not be suitable for everyone due to strict withdrawal rules and new taxes on high balances. Many Australians still face financial pressures that make super less appealing for younger age groups.
ticker_line: Superannuation gains but still not for all
author: ANTHONY KEANE
heading: Superannuation

title: Missing flood data hits 25,000
summary: Over 25,000 property owners in Logan have been informed that council flood maps over-estimated water levels by 1 metre. This finding, from an independent review, has raised concerns about property values and insurance costs.
ticker_line: Logan flood maps over-estimate water levels by 1m
author: Judith Kerr
heading: General Insurance

title: AI Data Centers Now Account for 17% of Australia’s Private Investment
summary: AI data centers now make up 17% of all private investment in Australia, highlighting the growing importance of artificial intelligence in the country's economy. This shift reflects a significant increase in funding and interest in AI technologies.
ticker_line: AI data centers surge to 17% of investment
heading: AI

title: The data centre mega-hub being quietly mapped out in Melbourne’s west
summary: A large data centre hub, called the Victorian AI Hub, is planned for Plumpton, Melbourne, covering 350 hectares and aiming for 2.4 gigawatts of power capacity. The project, developed by Syncline Energy, is expected to create thousands of jobs and support Victoria's tech industry, but it faces planning challenges due to its size and location.
ticker_line: Massive data centre planned for Melbourne's west
author: Daniella White
heading: AI

title: Macquarie could disrupt big four's 'lazy' $370b edge
summary: Macquarie is challenging Australia's big four banks by offering higher interest rates on savings, which could significantly impact the banks' profits. Analysts warn that if the major banks respond by raising rates, their earnings could drop by up to 40%.
ticker_line: Macquarie disrupts big banks with higher savings rates
author: Angira Bharadwaj
heading: Banking

title: Labor's new homes numbers $14b off RBA's
summary: The federal budget predicts $13.7 billion more investment in new housing than the Reserve Bank, suggesting the government may have overestimated housing supply by over 10,000 homes. This discrepancy raises concerns about meeting Labor's goal of 1.2 million new homes.
ticker_line: Budget housing investment forecasts raise concerns
author: Luke Kinsella
heading: Economy

title: HESTA backs $70m VC fund for med tech stars
summary: HESTA is investing $70 million in Synthesis Capital, a new venture capital firm focused on early-stage Australian health and medical technology start-ups. The fund aims to connect local scientists with global investors and address funding challenges in the sector.
ticker_line: HESTA invests $70m in med tech venture fund
author: Tess Bennett
heading: Investments

title: Social impact investors 'can fix NDIS'
summary: Michael Traill proposes a major change to the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) by consolidating its 270,000 providers into a few large operators backed by social impact investors to improve efficiency and save costs. This plan aims to address the scheme's financial issues and achieve $185 billion in budget savings over the next decade.
ticker_line: Traill's bold plan to reform NDIS funding
author: Michael Smith | Health
heading: General Insurance

title: Economic gap between older and younger generations on track to reach record levels
summary: A report shows the economic gap between younger and older Australians is growing, with younger people struggling more with home ownership and stagnant wages. This inequality could reach record levels in the coming years.
ticker_line: Economic gap between generations set to widen
author: business Adelaide Miller
heading: Economy

title: Bendigo deal on union’s hit list
summary: Bendigo Bank plans to transfer hundreds of tech workers to Infosys, but unions warn the new contracts could strip away basic rights and worsen pay conditions. The Finance Sector Union is advising workers not to sign these agreements.
ticker_line: Bendigo Bank's tech workers face contract concerns
author: Rishab Jain
heading: Banking

title: Rate hold expectations on slowdown
summary: Inflation in Australia slowed to 4.2% in April, partly due to the government's fuel excise cut, leading economists to expect the Reserve Bank to keep interest rates steady in June. However, rising costs in housing and transport, along with ongoing global issues, may continue to challenge inflation.
ticker_line: Inflation slows, interest rates likely to hold
author: Lea Jurkovic | Economics correspond
heading: Economy

title: Big fine for big four bank
summary: Westpac has been fined $26 million by the Federal Court for not properly handling over 200 customer requests for financial help between 2017 and 2023. The court found the bank was grossly negligent in its response to customers facing hardships like job loss and illness.
ticker_line: Westpac fined $26 million for customer neglect
author: David Hannant
heading: Banking

title: RBA’s dilemma: Fight inflation or save growth?
summary: Australia's economy is slowing down while inflation stays high, creating a tough situation for the Reserve Bank. With rising unemployment and concerns over new tax changes, the bank may need to decide soon whether to raise interest rates or keep them steady.
ticker_line: RBA faces tough choices amid slowing economy
author: David Rogers, markets
heading: Economy

title: Inflation dip may hold off rate rise
summary: Inflation in Australia has dropped to 4.2% in April, down from 4.6% in March, which may lead the Reserve Bank to pause interest rate hikes next month. However, ongoing global conflicts could still impact future inflation and rate decisions.
ticker_line: Inflation drop may pause interest rate hikes
author: Duncan Evans
heading: Economy

title: Audit reveals cyberattack fears over this year's census
summary: An audit has revealed that the Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS) needs to fix important cybersecurity issues before the upcoming census on August 11, 2026. The report highlights that while some improvements have been made, there are still critical gaps that could leave the system vulnerable to cyberattacks.
ticker_line: Census cybersecurity concerns raised ahead of August survey
author: Rob Harris | National correspondent
heading: AI

title: NSW teens’ tech literacy falling sharply
summary: IT literacy among 15 to 16-year-olds in NSW has dropped to record lows, with only 37% achieving a proficient standard in 2025, down from 44% in 2022. This decline reflects a national trend, despite many students reporting regular use of technology and AI.
ticker_line: NSW teens' IT skills hit record low
author: Will Paige and Mary Papadakis
heading: AI

title: Bosses failing to offer AI training for workers
summary: A survey shows that nearly 80% of workers using AI have not received any formal training from their employers, raising concerns about companies' readiness to adapt to new technologies. While many employees recognise the need for AI skills, employers are struggling to provide the necessary training and support.
ticker_line: Workers lack AI training as companies lag behind
author: EWIN HANNAN
heading: AI

title: House prices on road to nowhere, or ‘modest’ squeeze
summary: Macquarie analysts predict a possible decline in Australian house prices by 5% due to recent tax changes by the Albanese government, which may lead to a long period of stagnant prices. They warn that investor activity in the housing market could drop significantly, affecting new home lending and overall market turnover.
ticker_line: House prices face potential 5% decline
author: David Ross
heading: Economy

title: Australia:A sustainable CSLR needs structural reform, says Insurance Council
summary: The Insurance Council of Australia is urging for major changes to the Compensation Scheme of Last Resort (CSLR) to ensure its long-term sustainability, rather than just finding new funding methods. They believe reforms should focus on the root causes of costs and ensure that only those responsible for misconduct fund the compensation.
ticker_line: Insurance Council pushes for CSLR reform
author: Jimmy John
heading: General Insurance

title: Return to rural banking is a growth story for Westpac
summary: Westpac is reopening branches in rural Australia, including Moree, to better serve local communities and farmers. This move comes after years of branch closures, highlighting the importance of personal banking relationships in agriculture.
ticker_line: Westpac expands rural banking network for growth
author: Tim Johnston
heading: Banking

title: ASX hits the skids as costs up 21pc
summary: ASX shares fell 13% after the company announced a cost increase of up to 21% next year due to higher technology expenses and regulatory issues. This follows criticism from the Australian Securities and Investments Commission for prioritising profits over market stability.
ticker_line: ASX shares plunge as costs soar 21%
author: Cliona O’Dowd
heading: Banking

title: ‘No guarantees’ on Labor’s NDIS bill
summary: Opposition NDIS spokeswoman Melissa McIntosh warns there are 'no guarantees' the Coalition will support Labor's plan to cut $185 billion from the NDIS over the next decade, raising concerns about potential unfair cuts to participants. Any delays in passing the legislation could jeopardise the government's budget goals.
ticker_line: Coalition's support for NDIS cuts uncertain
author: Sarah Ison, Senior political report
heading: General Insurance

title: Data centres can wind up energy
summary: Energy Minister Chris Bowen believes Australia's data centre industry can help boost renewable energy projects, especially wind farms, as the country struggles to meet its 2030 renewable energy targets. With data centres expected to double their energy needs by 2030, new rules may be needed to ensure they contribute positively to the energy grid.
ticker_line: Data centres to aid Australia's renewable energy goals
author: Perry Williams, Elizabeth Pike
heading: AI

title: Tailored training and a trade focus means no one need be left behind
summary: AI has the potential to improve productivity for Australia's skilled tradespeople by reducing administrative tasks and enhancing safety, but it requires proper training and worker involvement for successful adoption.
ticker_line: AI could boost productivity for tradespeople
author: Steven Miller
heading: AI

title: AI’s economic gains proving elusive: Altman
summary: OpenAI's CEO Sam Altman says the expected economic benefits of artificial intelligence are harder to achieve than anticipated, impacting jobs in unexpected ways. He highlighted recent job cuts at companies like CBA and Block due to AI, while also noting Australia's potential as a global data centre hub.
ticker_line: AI's economic impact harder to achieve, says Altman
author: David Ross
heading: AI

title: Goodman upbeat on race for AI riches
summary: Billionaire Greg Goodman believes Australia can benefit from the global AI investment boom, which could be worth up to $1.4 trillion. He highlights the need for better infrastructure and affordable power to attract AI companies and meet growing demand for data centres.
ticker_line: Goodman optimistic about Australia's AI future
author: Ben Wilmot
heading: AI

title: Business council out of step with youth on AI
summary: Australia's business leaders warn that failing to prioritise AI and data centres could cost the economy $1.4 trillion, but many young people want more regulation due to job fears. The debate intensifies as major tech companies announce job cuts while investing heavily in AI infrastructure.
ticker_line: AI investment debate divides business and youth
author: Tansy Harcourt
heading: AI

title: Commonwealth Bank boss Matt Comyn says government should reconsider CGT on non-passive assets
summary: Commonwealth Bank CEO Matt Comyn has urged the government to limit capital gains tax changes to passive assets like housing, arguing that broader applications could discourage investment and innovation. He plans to discuss these concerns with Treasurer Jim Chalmers soon.
ticker_line: CBA CEO calls for targeted capital gains tax reform
heading: Banking

title: Investors on shaky ground as data points to a market slowdown
summary: Investors are facing challenges as new federal budget changes, including the removal of negative gearing for existing properties, are expected to reduce demand and lending in the housing market. Data shows a decline in housing loan commitments and a drop in consumer confidence, particularly among owner-occupiers.
ticker_line: Market slowdown as investor demand weakens
author: Nina Hendy
heading: Economy

title: Call to ban lead generators for super
summary: The Super Members Council is calling for a ban on lead generators in superannuation to protect Australians from fraud and unsafe financial practices. They urge the government to strengthen regulations and complete reforms to financial advice to ensure better safety for retirement savings.
ticker_line: Call to ban lead generators in superannuation
author: David Ross
heading: Superannuation

title: The AI experiment is over: Australian leaders now have to move
summary: Australian leaders must quickly decide which AI programs to scale up, as the trial phase is over and the country is lagging behind global trends in AI investment and adoption. Effective organisational change and clear standards are essential for successful AI integration.
ticker_line: Australian leaders urged to scale AI quickly
author: Bec McConnochie
heading: AI

title: Australia, UK tackle AI safety
summary: Australia and the UK have signed an agreement to share information on artificial intelligence risks, even before Australia establishes its own AI safety watchdog. This partnership aims to enhance research and responses to AI threats.
ticker_line: Australia and UK unite on AI safety measures
author: Jared Lynch
heading: AI

title: ASIC hails progress on red tape reduction
summary: The Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) has made significant progress in reducing red tape in financial services and is moving to the next phase of its simplification program. Recent changes include e-lodgement for forms and improved consultation with the industry to ease reporting burdens.
ticker_line: ASIC advances red tape reduction in finance
heading: Regulation

title: Global turmoil prompts APRA to step up supervision
summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) is increasing its supervision of banks, insurers, and superannuation funds due to global disruptions affecting supply chains and investment risks. While the financial system is stable, APRA is concerned about the growing links between private markets and regulated sectors, especially with life insurers investing in riskier assets.
ticker_line: APRA boosts oversight amid global financial risks
heading: Regulation

title: State maintains freeze on workers’ comp rates
summary: Victoria will keep its average WorkCover premium at 1.8% of wages for the fourth consecutive year in 2026-27. The government is focusing on improving support for injured workers, especially in high-risk jobs, without raising costs for businesses.
ticker_line: Victoria freezes workers' comp rates for 2026-27
heading: General Insurance

title: Home coverage warning as new smoke alarm rules loom
summary: Queensland homeowners must install new interconnected photoelectric smoke alarms by 2027 to keep their insurance valid. Failure to comply could lead to denied fire damage claims.
ticker_line: Queensland smoke alarm rules could impact insurance
heading: General Insurance

title: Three key innovation trends, according to ASIC
summary: ASIC's new report highlights three major trends in insurance innovation: the use of AI in claims and underwriting, the rise of embedded insurance products, and the growth of parametric and usage-based insurance. These trends are reshaping how insurance is distributed and managed in Australia.
ticker_line: ASIC outlines top trends in insurance innovation
heading: AI

title: Mortgage pain spreads to affluent suburbs
summary: Mortgage stress is now affecting wealthy suburbs as changes to property tax rules put pressure on homeowners and investors. This could lead to falling house prices and rising rents, especially in high-demand areas.
ticker_line: Mortgage stress hits wealthy suburbs hard
author: Ben Wilmot
heading: Banking

title: ‘We are risking a $1.4 trillion boom going bust’
summary: Australia risks missing out on a $1.4 trillion AI investment boom unless the government acts quickly to attract capital and streamline regulations, according to Business Council of Australia chief executive Bran Black. He urges a national commitment to make AI and data centre investments a priority to boost productivity and living standards.
ticker_line: Australia urged to seize $1.4 trillion AI opportunity
author: JOE KELLY, WASHINGTON CORRESPONDENT
heading: AI

title: Aussie bank helping secure ‘lifetime income’
summary: AMP is launching a new program called Lifetime Super Boost, allowing retirees to convert their superannuation into a steady income for life. This initiative aims to help Australians feel more secure about their retirement finances and potentially qualify for more aged pension support.
ticker_line: AMP launches program for secure retirement income
author: NewsWire
heading: Superannuation

title: Victoria’s data centre push could force state back onto gas-fired power
summary: Victoria's growing demand for data centres may force the state to rely more on gas-fired power, as local gas production declines and renewable projects face delays. This situation raises concerns about the state's energy transition plans and potential increases in electricity prices.
ticker_line: Victoria's data centres may increase gas reliance
author: Daniella White
heading: AI

title: TPD Sustainability Pressures Topic of Industry Debate
summary: Australia's financial regulators are urging life insurers to take stronger action on sustainability issues in Total Permanent Disability (TPD) insurance, following a recent industry debate. The discussion highlighted increasing mental health claims and the need for product redesign to improve affordability and manage risks.
ticker_line: Regulators push insurers on TPD sustainability challenges
heading: Life Insurance

title: Economy: Traders cut rate rise bets after jobs shock
summary: Traders are less sure the Reserve Bank of Australia will raise interest rates after a surprising rise in unemployment, which suggests the economy may be slowing. While some analysts predict more rate hikes due to ongoing inflation, others believe recent budget changes could reduce the need for further increases.
ticker_line: Traders doubt RBA will raise rates after jobs data
author: Cecile Lefort
heading: Economy

title: Bourse has best day in six weeks
summary: The Australian sharemarket had its best day in six weeks, with the S&P/ASX 200 rising 1.47% to 8621.7, driven by higher unemployment data and a positive lead from Wall Street. Mining stocks led the gains, while the unemployment rate increased to 4.5% in April, suggesting interest rates may stay steady.
ticker_line: Australian sharemarket sees biggest gain in weeks
author: BLAIR JACKSON, EQUITIES
heading: Economy

title: Jobless spike stalls RBA’s next rate hike, for now
summary: Australia's unemployment rate rose to 4.5% in April, leading the Reserve Bank to likely hold interest rates steady at its June meeting. Economists expect a rate hike may still happen by August, depending on future economic data.
ticker_line: Jobless rate rise delays RBA's next rate hike
author: DAVID ROGERS, MARKETS
heading: Economy

title: ASIC Warns of "Lost Generation" Risk if Australia Falls Behind on Fintech and AI
summary: ASIC warns that Australia could face a 'lost generation' if it doesn't keep up with global fintech and AI advancements. The regulator's report highlights the need for better industry engagement and innovation support to avoid a decline in living standards.
ticker_line: ASIC warns of risk to Australia's financial future
publication: Financial and Business News | Finance Magnates
author: Damian Chmiel
heading: Regulation

title: From SpaceX to OpenAI, Australians are piling into Wall St’s mega-deals
summary: Australians are increasingly investing in major US tech companies like SpaceX and OpenAI as local market options dwindle. SpaceX plans to go public in mid-June, potentially raising $80 billion, while interest in related investment funds is growing.
ticker_line: Australians flock to US tech investments
author: Alex Gluyas
heading: AI

title: RBA still stuck between a rock and a hike place
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia may hold interest rates steady next month after recent job losses suggest the economy is slowing, but inflation risks remain high due to ongoing global tensions and rising oil prices.
ticker_line: RBA may pause rate hikes amid job losses
author: James Thomson
heading: Economy

title: CSIRO cuts climate research jobs despite budget funding boost
summary: CSIRO will cut 92 jobs from its climate research teams despite receiving an extra $387.4 million in funding. Critics warn this could weaken Australia's ability to model and predict climate change impacts.
ticker_line: CSIRO cuts climate jobs despite funding boost
author: Bianca Hall and Mike Foley
heading: General Insurance

title: Negative gearing ban forces loan review
summary: Westpac has informed mortgage brokers that it will not honour pre-approved investor loans due to the federal government's ban on negative gearing for existing properties. This change is expected to reduce borrowing capacity for many investors by up to 20%.
ticker_line: Westpac reviews loans after negative gearing ban
author: Angira Bharadwaj
heading: Banking

title: AusSuper chair slams 'flawed' annual test
summary: Don Russell, chairman of AustralianSuper, criticises the annual performance test for super funds, calling it flawed and harmful to investment decisions. He supports proposed changes by Treasurer Jim Chalmers but believes a complete overhaul is necessary to encourage better investments for the economy.
ticker_line: Super fund test needs major overhaul, says Russell
author: Lucas Baird
heading: Superannuation

title: Buy local to build Aussie AI industry
summary: Australia is being urged to support local AI start-ups to strengthen its digital economy and ensure data sovereignty. Government officials stress that buying from Australian firms will help build expertise and competitiveness in the growing AI sector.
ticker_line: Support local AI firms to boost Australia's tech industry
author: Alexandra Cain
heading: AI

title: Australia leads rise of the small language model
summary: Australia is emerging as a leader in developing small language models (SLMs), which are more manageable and cost-effective than larger models. Businesses are increasingly recognising the benefits of creating their own SLMs using specialised data to gain a competitive edge.
ticker_line: Australia leads in small language model development
author: Alexandra Cain
heading: AI

title: CVs losing value for hiring as use of artificial intelligence changes industry, recruiters say
summary: The recruitment industry is changing as more job seekers and employers use artificial intelligence (AI), making traditional CVs less effective. Experts suggest that while AI helps in creating tailored applications, candidates must still showcase their unique skills and experiences.
ticker_line: AI reshapes job applications and recruitment process
heading: AI

title: ‘Brave assumptions’: FWC queries inflation forecasts
summary: The Fair Work Commission is questioning the government's inflation forecasts as it considers a wage rise for workers. The ACTU is pushing for a 6% increase, while the government suggests a rise below 5% could still be acceptable.
ticker_line: FWC questions government inflation forecasts for wage rise
author: EWIN HANNAN
heading: Economy

title: Climate inaction to force up rates: Kean
summary: Matt Kean, Australia's climate change tsar, warns that failing to address climate change will lead to permanently higher interest rates and lower household incomes. A report shows that the economic impact of climate inaction will be severe, particularly in NSW and Queensland.
ticker_line: Climate inaction could raise interest rates permanently
author: ROSIE LEWIS, NATIONAL AFFAIRS CORRE
heading: General Insurance

title: Allianz Partners' nib deal signals a new era for ANZ travel distribution
summary: Allianz Partners has acquired nib Group's travel insurance portfolio, including the Travel Insurance Direct brand, in a deal worth up to AUD$50 million. This move marks Allianz's return to the face-to-face travel insurance market, which is significant as nearly a third of Australian travel insurance is still sold through physical agents.
ticker_line: Allianz Partners buys nib's travel insurance portfolio
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/daniel-wood/
heading: General Insurance

title: Allianz claims top spot as gap between insurers opens up over successful AI competitive advantage
summary: Allianz has topped the 2026 AI Index for insurers, surpassing AXA due to significant improvements in talent and technology. The report highlights a growing gap between leading insurers and others, with AI becoming crucial for competitive advantage in the industry.
ticker_line: Allianz leads AI rankings, widening insurer gap
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/matthew-sellers/
heading: General Insurance

title: PwC's AI barometer says insurance expertise wins
summary: PwC's report shows that AI is boosting productivity in professional roles, especially in insurance, but Australian companies must also meet strict regulatory standards from APRA and ASIC to succeed. The report highlights that firms with strong AI governance are more likely to achieve significant productivity gains.
ticker_line: AI boosts productivity but raises regulatory concerns
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/matthew-sellers/
heading: General Insurance

title: ASIC holds off on SPO licensing but attaches conditions
summary: ASIC has extended its no-action position for second party opinion providers in Australia's sustainability finance market until June 2028, allowing them to operate without a full licensing framework. This extension comes with conditions, including that the reports must not be used for retail clients.
ticker_line: ASIC extends no-action position for SPO providers
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: Regulation

title: ACIL calls out insurers before Parliament over affordability crisis
summary: The Australian Consumers Insurance Lobby (ACIL) has urged Parliament to hold insurers accountable for the ongoing insurance affordability crisis, claiming that insurers often blame external factors instead of addressing their own responsibilities. The inquiry highlights issues in various sectors, with calls for legislative action to improve access to essential insurance services for businesses and communities.
ticker_line: ACIL demands action on insurance affordability crisis
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: General Insurance

title: All-round digital asset service wanted
summary: SMSF trustees want reliable service providers for investing in digital assets like cryptocurrency, seeking solutions that simplify compliance and administration. Currently, less than 0.5% of the $1 trillion in SMSF assets are in digital assets, indicating growth potential for service providers.
ticker_line: SMSF trustees seek better digital asset services
author: https://www.facebook.com/smstrusteenews
heading: Superannuation

title: Trend to younger SMSF members
summary: The latest ATO report shows that nearly 40% of new Self-Managed Super Fund (SMSF) members are under 45, indicating a trend towards younger investors. In total, SMSF numbers grew to 672,805 funds, with 1.234 million members as of March 2026.
ticker_line: Younger investors flock to Self-Managed Super Funds
author: https://www.facebook.com/smstrusteenews
heading: Superannuation

title: Regulators push for stronger stress-testing capability, resilience
summary: APRA and ASIC are urging superannuation funds to improve their ability to handle financial stress and manage liquidity risks. Recent discussions highlighted the need for better oversight of service providers and targeted stress-testing to enhance resilience in the sector.
ticker_line: Regulators call for stronger superannuation stress tests
author: Karren Vergara
heading: Superannuation

title: ASIC Daily News round-up - 16 June 2026
summary: ASIC and APRA are making changes to the Financial Accountability Regime to lessen the regulatory burden on businesses while maintaining accountability. Additionally, ASIC has extended a no-action position for certain financial advice providers who do not hold an AFS licence when advising wholesale clients.
ticker_line: ASIC and APRA ease Financial Accountability rules
author: "ASIC Media Unit" <media@newsletters.asic.gov.au>
heading: ASIC

title: APRA targets super fund boards with governance overhaul
summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) is proposing new governance rules for superannuation fund boards to improve oversight and reduce compliance burdens. The changes aim to ensure that boards have capable leaders and streamline reporting requirements, with new rules expected to take effect in early 2028.
ticker_line: APRA proposes new governance rules for super funds
author: Adrian Suljanovic
heading: Superannuation

title: Super funds lagging renewable investments, research finds
summary: Australia's largest super funds have invested only 4% of the renewable energy needed to meet the government's 2030 climate targets, raising concerns about their role in the energy transition. The report suggests that super funds should increase their investments in clean energy to benefit members and the economy.
ticker_line: Super funds fall short on renewable energy investments
author: Adrian Suljanovic
heading: Superannuation

title: HESTA extends J.P. Morgan custody partnership for 5 years
summary: HESTA has extended its partnership with J.P. Morgan for custody and fund services for another five years, following a review. This agreement will help HESTA manage its $102 billion in member funds and continue delivering strong investment returns.
ticker_line: HESTA renews J.P. Morgan custody deal for five years
author: Adrian Suljanovic
heading: Superannuation

title: APRA and ASIC announce FAR changes to reduce administrative burden
summary: APRA and ASIC are making changes to the Financial Accountability Regime to reduce the reporting burden for businesses. These changes will simplify requirements and are expected to be implemented by the end of 2026.
ticker_line: APRA and ASIC simplify Financial Accountability Regime
author: APRA web updates <subscriptions@apra.gov.au>
heading: APRA

title: APRA commences next phase of push to strengthen and streamline governance requirements
summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) is updating its governance rules for banks, superannuation, and insurance to improve leadership quality and reduce unnecessary reporting. The new rules aim to enhance board oversight and efficiency, with feedback being collected until the end of August 2026, and the changes expected to take effect in early 2028.
ticker_line: APRA updates governance rules for financial sectors
author: APRA web updates <subscriptions@apra.gov.au>
heading: APRA

title: Media release: Airlines increase airfares and reduce flights on some routes as fuel costs rise
summary: Australia's domestic airlines are raising airfares and cutting flights on some routes due to rising jet fuel prices, according to the ACCC's latest report. This change is expected to affect travel options for passengers.
ticker_line: Airlines hike fares, cut flights amid fuel costs rise
author: ACCC Media <do-not-reply@accc.gov.au>
heading: ACCC

title: IAG invests in platform delivering immediate mental health support
summary: Insurance Australia Group (IAG) has invested in Sonder, a platform that offers immediate mental health support, aiming to help policyholders during major incidents like floods and cyclones. This move is part of IAG's strategy to provide earlier intervention and support beyond traditional insurance claims.
ticker_line: IAG invests in mental health support platform Sonder
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: General Insurance

title: Media release: Grill'd in court for allegedly misleading customers about 'Tree Day Tuesday' donations
summary: The ACCC is taking Grill'd to court for allegedly misleading customers about its 'Tree Day Tuesday' donations, claiming the company falsely stated it would donate $1 from each burger sold on Tuesdays to plant trees. This case is based on actions between January 2021 and April 2024, which the ACCC says violates Australian Consumer Law.
ticker_line: Grill'd faces court over misleading donations
author: ACCC Media <do-not-reply@accc.gov.au>
heading: ACCC

title: Marsh strikes deals with five insurers to simplify cyber payouts
summary: Marsh Risk has launched a new framework with five insurers to speed up cyber insurance payouts, allowing for advance payments and clearer guidelines on claims. This aims to reduce costs and simplify the claims process for businesses affected by cyber incidents.
ticker_line: Marsh simplifies cyber insurance payouts with new framework
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/mark-rosanes/
heading: General Insurance

title: Flood-hit Bundaberg faces an insurance market in retreat
summary: Bundaberg, Queensland, is facing a crisis as home insurance becomes unaffordable after severe flooding. The local council rejected a $175 million levee project, leading to increased premiums and many residents lacking flood coverage.
ticker_line: Bundaberg faces rising insurance costs after floods
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: General Insurance

title: Old processes driving member change
summary: Many Australians are leaving their super funds for alternatives like SMSFs due to outdated paper processes and poor user experience. Experts say super funds need to improve their services to keep members engaged and reduce outflows.
ticker_line: Super funds face member losses to SMSFs
author: https://www.facebook.com/smstrusteenews
heading: Superannuation

title: Home insurance is in structural crisis and APRA numbers tell you exactly how bad it is
summary: Australia's home insurance market is facing a crisis, with premiums rising sharply but insurers still reporting significant losses due to extreme weather events and rising building costs. Many homeowners are becoming uninsured or underinsured as affordability issues grow.
ticker_line: Home insurance crisis worsens amid rising costs
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/daniel-wood/
heading: General Insurance

title: ASFA lifts comfortable retirement standard to $730k for couples
summary: The Association of Superannuation Funds of Australia (ASFA) has raised the comfortable retirement savings benchmark to $730,000 for couples and $630,000 for singles, while many Australians believe they will need over $1 million to retire comfortably. Rising costs due to inflation and housing affordability issues are influencing these perceptions.
ticker_line: ASFA raises retirement savings benchmarks significantly
author: Karren Vergara
heading: Superannuation

title: Willis bets on data to close property insurance's growing climate gap
summary: Willis is improving its Climate Diagnostic tool to help property insurers better understand climate risks and protect assets from extreme weather. This comes as global insurance losses from natural disasters rise, with many properties underinsured, especially in the UK.
ticker_line: Willis enhances tools to tackle climate insurance risks
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/uk/authors/mark-rosanes/
heading: General Insurance

title: ASIC Daily News round-up - 15 June 2026
summary: ASIC has permanently banned Abdullah Popal from financial services due to fraud convictions. Additionally, ASX admitted to misleading the market about its CHESS replacement project, which could have caused financial harm.

title: Four in ten overestimate retirement savings needed
summary: A new poll shows that 42% of Australians think they need over $1 million in superannuation for a comfortable retirement, while actual benchmarks are $730,000 for couples and $630,000 for singles. Rising living costs and housing affordability concerns are influencing these beliefs, especially among younger Australians.

heading: Superannuation

summary: Ugg boot retailer Christofi Investments has paid $39,600 in penalties for misleading pricing claims on its websites. The ACCC found that the company advertised discounts alongside inflated original prices, which were not accurate.

title: Where AI meets PI: Coverage questions the industry is still answering

They note that traditional insurance products may not adequately cover new risks associated with AI.
ticker_line: AI reshapes professional indemnity insurance landscape

heading: ACCC

summary: Victoria is close to making insurance for apartment defects mandatory, requiring developers to have a 10-year policy before occupancy permits are granted. The bill has passed the Legislative Assembly and will be debated in the Legislative Council soon.

summary: Many superannuation trustees in Australia are struggling to improve their death benefit claims processes, which is causing delays for grieving families. AFCA authorised as External Dispute Resolution service for Scams

summary: Older Australians aged 55 to 75 made up half of all complaints to super funds in 2025, with the most complaints from those aged 55 to 64. summary: Australian super funds are facing challenges with complex investments, liquidity demands, and the use of technology like AI, according to a new survey. As they invest more in private markets, managing risks and data has become crucial for their strategies.

summary: The Super Members Council is asking the government to reject plans that would make superannuation funds pay for the Compensation Scheme of Last Resort, arguing that those responsible for financial misconduct should bear the costs instead. title: Medical imaging provider to investigate improper Medicare billing allegations
summary: Integral Diagnostics will investigate claims of improper Medicare billing at Kalgoorlie Health Campus after concerns were raised about incorrect practices. The WA health minister has referred the matter to the state's corruption watchdog for further examination.
ticker_line: Investigation into Medicare billing practices launched
heading: Health Insurance

title: ‘Gut punch’ warning over the new rates reality
summary: Australian mortgage holders face permanently higher interest rates due to ongoing economic shocks, according to Oxford Economics. Despite recent inflation drops, the Reserve Bank is expected to keep rates steady for now as the economy slows.
ticker_line: Mortgage holders brace for higher interest rates
author: Cameron Micallef
heading: Economy

title: Minister on board to slow AI juggernaut
summary: The Australian government supports Anthropic's call for a global pause in AI development to address safety concerns, as the company warns of risks from advanced AI that can improve itself. Assistant Minister Andrew Charlton emphasizes the need for careful regulation and safety measures as AI technology rapidly evolves.
ticker_line: Government backs Anthropic's AI safety push
author: Jared Lynch
heading: AI

title: Australians less satisfied with life than during pandemic as financial pressures mount
summary: Australians are feeling less satisfied with life now than during the COVID-19 pandemic, mainly due to rising financial pressures. Data shows life satisfaction dropped from 7.2 out of 10 in 2020 to 7.1 last year, with many struggling to manage costs and save money.
ticker_line: Australians report lower life satisfaction amid financial stress
author: business Daniel Ziffer
heading: Economy

title: Long delays leave Tasmanians waiting for surgery amid worries about $700m in health cuts
summary: Tasmania's elective surgery waitlist has reached about 9,500 people, with many waiting years for procedures. The state government plans to cut $702 million from health services over four years, raising concerns about the impact on patient care and service delivery.
ticker_line: Tasmania faces health cuts amid surgery delays
author: state political Josh Dugga
heading: Health

title: Data centre boom towns ride the economic wave, but can it last?
summary: Victoria and NSW are experiencing a massive data centre boom, investing nearly $300 billion, surpassing the previous mining boom. However, experts warn that this growth may not lead to long-term economic benefits for Australians and could face challenges ahead.
ticker_line: Data centre boom surpasses mining investment in Australia
author: Shane Wright | Senior economics cor
heading: Economy

title: Westpac extends ‘DIDO’ banking
summary: Westpac is expanding its drive-in-drive-out banking service to six new towns in Queensland, aiming to provide in-person banking in areas without branches. The bank will also invest $10 million to improve branches in regional Queensland.
ticker_line: Westpac expands DIDO banking to Queensland towns
author: David Ross
heading: Banking

title: NEW RESEARCH REVEALS HIDDEN SCALE OF CHILD HOMELESSNESS
summary: New research shows over 33,000 children in Aotearoa face severe housing deprivation, leading to higher risks of illness, school absenteeism, and contact with the justice system. The Coalition to End Women’s Homelessness emphasizes the need for housing solutions, especially for mothers, to protect these vulnerable children.
ticker_line: Over 33,000 children face severe housing issues
author: Amelia Wade
heading: General Insurance

The Coalition to End Women’s Homelessness highlights the need for solutions that also support mothers to improve children's living conditions.
ticker_line: 33,000 children in Aotearoa face homelessness crisis

ticker_line: Over 33,000 children face severe housing issues
author: Amelia Wade
heading: General Insurance

title: Australia’s data centre explosion: Why the surge in NSW, Victoria is now eclipsing the mining boom
summary: NSW and Victoria are experiencing a massive surge in data centre investments, surpassing the previous mining boom with nearly $300 billion spent in the past year. While this growth is expected to boost the economy, concerns remain about its long-term benefits and potential risks.
ticker_line: Data centre boom outpaces mining investment in Australia
author: Shane Wright
heading: Economy

title: Strata giant unmasked in secret lobbying to save insurance kickbacks
summary: PICA Group, Australia's largest strata company, secretly lobbied against a ban on insurance kickbacks, claiming it would threaten 70% of strata management firms. The government has not yet banned these kickbacks but is considering further studies on the issue.
ticker_line: PICA Group lobbies against insurance kickback ban
author: Rachael Dexter
heading: Regulation

title: AI is coming for these 82 Aussie jobs
summary: Deloitte warns that 82 Australian jobs are at high risk from AI disruption in 2026, predicting job growth will slow significantly due to economic conditions and AI advancements. While some companies have already cut jobs, overall employment in affected sectors is still growing, though at a reduced rate.
ticker_line: Deloitte warns of AI job risks in Australia
author: Heath Parkes-Hupton
heading: AI

title: Victorian teachers in class action over alleged unpaid superannuation
summary: Victorian teachers, led by Jill Perkins, are suing the Department of Education for unpaid superannuation on a salary allowance from 1995 to 2023, claiming a total loss of $400 million. The Australian Education Union argues this payment should have included superannuation contributions, which the department is currently appealing in court.
ticker_line: Victorian teachers sue for unpaid superannuation
author: story by Sacha Payne
heading: Superannuation

title: Federal government accused of AI policy retreat as US tech giants plan Australian investments
summary: The Australian government has backed away from strict AI regulations, which has raised concerns about safety as major tech companies plan significant investments in the country. Critics argue that the decision could have serious implications for consumer protection and privacy.
ticker_line: Government retreats on AI safety regulations
author: Steve Cannane, Lesley Robinson, Lar
heading: Regulation

title: ANZ’s fixed rate shock
summary: ANZ has reduced its two and three-year fixed home loan rates by up to 0.10 percentage points, with the lowest rate now at 6.29%. This move is unusual as most major banks have been increasing their fixed rates recently.
ticker_line: ANZ cuts fixed home loan rates amid rising trends
heading: Banking

title: Anthropic urges AI freeze amid fear of losing control
summary: Anthropic, the top AI company, has called for a global pause on developing powerful AI systems, warning that humanity risks losing control. They are concerned about rapid advancements that could lead to dangerous outcomes and suggest that all major AI firms should agree to slow down their work.
ticker_line: Anthropic calls for global AI development freeze
author: David Swan | Technology
heading: AI

title: Business banking on boost from AI
summary: Australian business leaders, including executives from BHP and Uber, discussed how adopting artificial intelligence (AI) could improve productivity despite some challenges. They highlighted AI's potential to prevent costly shutdowns and create new job roles, while also acknowledging the current low productivity levels in Australia since the Covid-19 pandemic.
ticker_line: AI seen as key to boosting productivity
author: Jared Lynch, Perry Williams
heading: Economy

title: The one age at which you will pay more tax than any other
summary: People aged 43 pay the most tax in Australia, averaging $59,000 a year. This age group faces high taxes due to higher incomes and family expenses, while younger and older Australians benefit more from government spending.
ticker_line: Age 43 sees peak tax burden in Australia
author: Anthony Keane
heading: Economy

title: Aussies pull out $13 billion, huge super move
summary: Over the past year, Australians have withdrawn more than $13 billion from large super funds to invest in self-managed super funds (SMSFs), nearly doubling the trend from the previous year. While SMSFs offer more control over investments, they come with significant risks and responsibilities that many may not fully understand.
ticker_line: Aussies withdraw $13 billion for self-managed super funds
author: Ben Graham
heading: Superannuation

title: Treasury: No boost to growth from budget
summary: Treasury reports that the Albanese government's major tax changes won't boost economic growth or productivity, focusing instead on wealth redistribution. Despite some targeted measures for small businesses, overall forecasts for growth remain unchanged.
ticker_line: Treasury says budget won't boost economic growth
author: John Kehoe | Economics
heading: Economy

title: CBA offers staff $3000 a head to recommend private bank recruits
summary: Commonwealth Bank is offering staff $3000 for each successful referral of new hires to its private bank until June 30, as competition for skilled workers intensifies. This follows a trend of major banks, including Westpac and NAB, actively recruiting talent from each other.
ticker_line: CBA pays $3000 for staff recruitment referrals
author: Angira Bharadwaj
heading: Banking

title: 'Complacent' super giants lose $8b in a year
summary: Australians moved nearly $8 billion from large super funds to self-managed super funds (SMSFs) in the past year, highlighting a growing trend of disengagement with traditional pension funds. Major funds like AustralianSuper and Australian Retirement Trust are facing increased member losses as more people seek personalized financial advice and control over their investments.
ticker_line: Australians shift $8 billion to SMSFs
author: Lucas Baird
heading: Superannuation

title: Negative equity looms for recent homebuyers
summary: Commonwealth Bank predicts falling house prices in Sydney and Melbourne, which could lead to negative equity for recent buyers. Other cities like Perth and Brisbane are expected to see price increases.
ticker_line: House price drops may hit recent buyers hard
author: David Rogers, markets
heading: Economy

title: Jobs harder to find but AI not to blame
summary: A Deloitte report shows that finding a job in Australia will be tougher in 2026, but this is not mainly due to AI. Instead, rising interest rates and economic issues from the Middle East conflict are affecting hiring.
ticker_line: Job market tightens amid economic uncertainty
heading: AI

title: There’s one age you’ll pay more tax than any other
summary: People aged 43 pay the most tax in Australia, averaging $59,000 a year, due to high incomes and spending. Recent government budget changes are expected to increase tax burdens on younger and middle-aged Australians while benefiting older investors.
ticker_line: Age 43 sees peak tax burden in Australia
author: Anthony Keane
heading: Economy

title: New super rules spark $6bn small business cash crisis
summary: New rules for superannuation payments, starting July 1, require Australian employers to pay super at the same time as wages, potentially forcing small businesses to find an extra $6 billion and dip into personal savings due to cash flow issues from late customer payments.
ticker_line: New super rules may strain small business cash flow
author: Rishab Jain
heading: Superannuation

title: Unfair health insurance rebate cut will ripple through the generations
summary: The Albanese government plans to cut health insurance rebates for older Australians, arguing for fairness between generations. However, this decision may lead to more financial and emotional strain on families as older Australians could drop their coverage, resulting in longer public hospital waiting times and increased care responsibilities for their adult children.
ticker_line: Health insurance rebate cuts impact families deeply
author: DR RACHEL DAVID
heading: Health Insurance

title: Labor’s planned NDIS overhaul is ‘blunt and inequitable’, thinktank says
summary: The Grattan Institute criticises Labor's plan to cut funding for social participation in the NDIS by 50%, calling it unfair and poorly thought out. The government aims to reduce costs in the $50 billion scheme, but the changes may harm those with disabilities who rely on these supports.
ticker_line: NDIS funding cuts face strong criticism from experts
author: Sarah Basford Canales
heading: General Insurance

title: Unexpected AI boom contributed to RBA interest rate U-turn
summary: RBA Governor Michele Bullock says the unexpected growth in artificial intelligence (AI) has boosted the global economy, leading the Reserve Bank to change its plans on interest rates. Despite earlier expectations of an economic slowdown, strong AI investment has driven growth in Australia and its trading partners.
ticker_line: AI boom prompts RBA interest rate changes
author: Lea Jurkovic
heading: Economy

title: RBA boss says economy can weather war and avoid recession
summary: Reserve Bank governor Michele Bullock believes Australia can avoid a recession despite rising oil prices due to the ongoing war. She stated that the economy is expected to grow, with strong investment in sectors like software and renewable energy.
ticker_line: RBA optimistic about economy amid rising oil prices
author: Shane Wright
heading: Economy

title: Car theft falls in every state but one and insurers are angry
summary: Car theft claims in Victoria have surged by 25%, reaching $243 million, while other states saw declines. Insurers are upset as the rise in thefts is linked to increased crime concerns in the state ahead of the upcoming election.
ticker_line: Victoria sees major rise in car theft claims
author: Patrick Durkin
heading: General Insurance

title: 20pc house price fall will open doors for investors
summary: Morgan Stanley's chief economist Chris Read says house prices need to fall by 15-20% to attract investors back into the housing market after recent tax changes. He warns that lower prices and higher rental yields are necessary for investors to regain previous returns.
ticker_line: House prices must drop to attract investors
author: DAVID ROGERS, MARKETS
heading: Economy

title: Bourse jumps as rate fears ease
summary: Australia's sharemarket rose as hopes grew that the Reserve Bank will keep interest rates steady. BHP and Rio Tinto reached record highs, while the economy showed slower growth, reducing the chance of further rate hikes.
ticker_line: Australian sharemarket rebounds on interest rate hopes
author: Cameron Micallef, Equities
heading: Economy

title: Stagflation fears rise as OECD lowers Australian forecasts
summary: Australia faces stagflation risks as the OECD lowers economic growth forecasts, predicting inflation to reach 5% and GDP growth to slow significantly. The country is experiencing its longest per-capita recession in over 65 years, driven by rising interest rates and reduced consumer spending.
ticker_line: Stagflation fears rise as OECD cuts forecasts
author: MATTHEW CRANSTON
heading: Economy

title: Mercer Super agrees to pay $10.3m fine to ASIC
summary: Mercer Super has agreed to pay a $10.3 million fine to the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) for failing to report serious issues with member services. The fund also faces $1.2 million in legal costs and has admitted to not reporting seven internal investigations between 2021 and 2024.
ticker_line: Mercer Super hit with $10.3 million fine
author: Angelica Snowden
heading: Regulation

title: Technology: 'Dangerous' AI program to hamper the hackers
summary: Australian organisations will soon access Anthropic's powerful AI model, Claude Mythos, which can identify software flaws that hackers might exploit. This move is part of a global effort to strengthen cybersecurity for critical systems, especially in sectors like finance and infrastructure.
ticker_line: Australia gains access to powerful AI for cybersecurity
author: David Swan
heading: AI

title: Australia's economic slowdown is just beginning, economists warn, as recession risks rise
summary: Australia's economy is slowing down, with growth at just 0.3% in early 2026 and rising unemployment. Economists warn of a possible recession as inflation remains high and consumer spending weakens.
ticker_line: Australia faces rising recession risks amid economic slowdown
author: business correspondent David Taylor
heading: Economy

title: Seniors centre leak fears
summary: Mosman Council is investigating a data breach that may have exposed personal information of hundreds of residents linked to the Mosman Square Seniors Centre. The breach occurred through a third-party software system used for community services, and an external cybersecurity firm has been hired to assess the situation.
ticker_line: Data breach at Mosman Council raises concerns
author: Jim O’Rourke
heading: AI

title: Australia gains access to powerful AI amid questions about readiness
summary: Australia has gained access to a powerful AI system called Mythos, which is designed to improve cybersecurity. The government is under pressure to act faster on AI risks and opportunities as independent MPs raise concerns about the pace of its response.
ticker_line: Australia gains access to advanced AI for cybersecurity
author: chief digital political corresponde
heading: AI

title: AI may be tightening jobs market, but some roles could see stronger demand
summary: A Deloitte report highlights that while AI is changing the job market, it may not lead to widespread job losses. Some roles, especially those requiring human skills like creativity and empathy, could see increased demand as AI complements their work.
ticker_line: AI reshapes job market, some roles thrive
author: national work Bronwyn Herb
heading: AI

title: First trade deficit since 2017 opens ‘possible’ contraction
summary: Australia has recorded its first trade deficit since 2017, prompting major banks to lower their economic growth forecasts. This could lead to the first quarterly contraction in economic activity since the COVID pandemic, with concerns over rising imports and falling exports.
ticker_line: Australia faces first trade deficit since 2017
author: Noah Yim, Matthew Cranston
heading: Economy

title: Exclusive: RBA thinks reforms will help cut inflation
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) believes that Labor's tax reforms and falling consumer confidence may lower house prices and spending, which could help reduce inflation. This comes as the government faces criticism over its housing policies and prepares for a key interest rate decision on June 16.
ticker_line: RBA sees potential inflation relief from tax reforms
author: Paul Sakkal and Shane Wright
heading: Economy

title: Wages rise, rates to follow
summary: Australia's minimum wage will rise by 4.75% to $26.44 an hour from July 1, which may lead to two interest rate hikes later this year due to ongoing inflation and low productivity. Business leaders warn this wage increase could cause financial strain and a wave of insolvencies in the hospitality sector.
ticker_line: Minimum wage rise may trigger interest rate hikes
author: Jessica Wang
heading: Economy

title: Minister eases AI burden on FWC
summary: Minister Amanda Rishworth has proposed changes to help the Fair Work Commission manage its growing workload from AI-related claims. The new bill aims to speed up processes, reduce unnecessary hearings, and improve support for workers and businesses.
ticker_line: Fair Work Commission workload eased by new bill
author: EWIN HANNAN
heading: AI

title: CommBank readies ‘Can’ brand evolution with new agency appointment
summary: Commonwealth Bank is changing its brand strategy by appointing a new advertising agency, Howatson+Company, to refresh its long-standing 'Can' campaign. This shift aims to strengthen its market leadership and adapt to evolving customer needs and technology.
ticker_line: CommBank evolves brand with new agency appointment
author: Danielle Long
heading: Banking

title: Exclusive: RBA rates Labor bad on homes
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) has criticized Labor's housing policies, saying they have not improved home supply and may have raised prices. Labor's plans to increase housing availability are under scrutiny as migration rates and other factors continue to impact affordability.
ticker_line: RBA criticizes Labor's housing policies and impact
author: Paul Sakkal | Chief political corre
heading: Economy

title: CEOs get AI twins as clones step up
summary: Australian CEOs are using AI to create digital versions of themselves, helping with tasks like writing memos and managing schedules. This trend is changing corporate communications, but experts warn that human skills are still needed for effective messaging.
ticker_line: CEOs embrace AI clones for corporate tasks
author: Jared Lynch
heading: AI

title: Treasury sets out industry’s APRA levies
summary: Treasury plans to collect about $27.2 million from the general insurance industry to support the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) in the next financial year. The overall levy for APRA is estimated at $297.3 million, which includes costs for life insurance as well.
ticker_line: Treasury outlines APRA levies for insurance sector
heading: Regulation

title: Beware knock-on effects of mental health reform: EML
summary: A report from EML warns that changes to mental health support in workers' compensation could lead to more claims in income protection insurance. It calls for better coordination across Australia's support systems to prevent financial vulnerability and improve recovery outcomes.
ticker_line: EML warns of mental health reform impacts
heading: Life Insurance

title: Ombudsman slams icare over wage adjustment delays
summary: The NSW Ombudsman has criticized icare for delays in processing wage adjustments for workers' compensation, which caused financial issues for employers. The Ombudsman recommends icare apologize, offer flexible payment plans, and improve its processes.
ticker_line: Ombudsman criticizes icare for wage adjustment delays
heading: General Insurance

title: NSW confirms workers’ comp premiums
summary: NSW employers will soon get workers' compensation renewal notices, with icare confirming premium settings. The minimum premium stays at $240, and the cap on premium increases for some employers will drop from 30% to 25%.
ticker_line: NSW confirms workers' comp premium changes
heading: General Insurance

title: Perils lifts November storms loss tally
summary: Storms in Queensland and NSW last November caused insured losses of $2.94 billion, according to Swiss data company Perils. The updated figure shows an increase from the previous estimate of $2.66 billion, with most losses coming from Queensland.
ticker_line: November storms cost $2.94 billion in losses
heading: General Insurance

title: Insurers to revisit storm-hit Clifton
summary: Insurers will hold support sessions in Clifton, Queensland on June 8-9 for residents affected by a hailstorm last November. Policyholders can meet with insurer representatives to get help with claims and recovery.
ticker_line: Insurers offer support for storm-hit Clifton residents
heading: General Insurance

title: Industry profit down amid investment slump
summary: The life insurance industry reported a profit of $121 million for the March quarter, a significant drop from $332 million a year earlier, due to lower investment returns and increased claims. Underwriting profits also fell, with individual disability insurance performing best among product classes.
ticker_line: Life insurance profits decline amid investment slump
heading: Life Insurance

title: Housing price slump in Melbourne continues
summary: Melbourne and Sydney are seeing significant drops in housing prices due to high interest rates and new tax changes. In May, Sydney house values fell to $1.58 million, while Melbourne's median house price dropped to $958,000.
ticker_line: Housing prices continue to fall in Melbourne and Sydney
author: Shane Wright and Matthew Knott
heading: Economy

title: Retirement anxiety grows
summary: Many Australians are delaying retirement by up to four years due to rising living costs, with most expecting to work until 66 instead of their desired 62. A new report shows that people now believe they need over $1 million in superannuation for a comfortable retirement.
ticker_line: Australians delay retirement amid rising costs
author: Cameron Micallef
heading: Superannuation

title: Westpac audit chair sat in on pitch talks despite KPMG conflict fears
summary: Westpac director Peter Nash attended pitch meetings for the bank's $32 million audit despite concerns about conflicts of interest due to his past role at KPMG. This has raised questions about Westpac's earlier claims that he was not involved in the tender process.
ticker_line: Westpac audit pitch raises conflict of interest concerns
heading: Banking
